hero_headline: States are writing the rules. Districts are learning the lessons.
intro: This month leans practical: where state AI regulation is heading, what district leaders wish they had done differently, and the watch-outs worth slowing down for. Skim it, click what is useful, ignore the rest. That is the deal.

editor_pick.title: A Washington district saved $250K by building its own tools with AI
editor_pick.blurb: Instead of buying another ed-tech subscription, they "vibe coded" what they needed. This is the operations story I find most interesting right now, because the savings are real and the barrier to entry keeps dropping.

sections.name: Leading & Policy
sections.sub: For the people making the calls

items.title: 3 Trends Emerge as States Race to Regulate AI in K-12
items.takeaway: Where state AI rules are heading. Useful for staying ahead of whatever Tennessee lands on.

items.title: 3 Mistakes District Leaders Say They've Made Rolling Out AI
items.takeaway: Hard-won lessons from leaders who went first, so we can skip the avoidable stumbles.

items.title: How the Education Department Will Prioritize AI in Grants
items.takeaway: Federal grant dollars are starting to favor AI work. Handy if we go chasing funding.

items.title: CoSN 2026: Start AI Strategy With Goals for Student Skills
items.takeaway: Lead with the skills you want students to leave with, not the shiny tool. Sound advice.

sections.name: In the Classroom
sections.sub: Teaching, learning, and the students themselves

items.title: Students Took a Deep Dive Into AI. Here's What They Told Teachers
items.takeaway: Students describe what good and bad AI use actually looks like from their seat. Read it before you assume.

items.title: The Future of AI in the Classroom
items.takeaway: A grounded look at where this is heading, minus the hype in either direction.

items.title: The Quest to Build a Better AI Tutor
items.takeaway: What it actually takes to make an AI tutor that helps kids learn rather than just answer for them.

items.title: Flint: A K-12 Personalized Learning Platform
items.takeaway: One to watch for personalized learning. See what is new and whether it fits a classroom you know.

items.title: Gemini in Google Classroom Now in All Supported Languages
items.takeaway: Now available across every supported language, which matters for our multilingual families.

sections.name: Proceed With Caution
sections.sub: The other side of the conversation
sections.note: A standing spot for the honest watch-outs. AI is a tool, not a shortcut around judgment, and these are the limits and risks worth keeping in front of us.

items.title: NYC Schools Prohibit AI for Grading, Discipline, and IEPs
items.takeaway: The country's largest district drew hard red lines around the decisions that affect kids most. A useful reference for where we draw ours.

items.title: AI Is Changing Teacher Hiring. Here's How
items.takeaway: AI is quietly screening applications and scoring interviews. Worth understanding before it filters out a teacher we would have wanted to meet.

items.title: Education, Social Cohesion, and Democracy in the Age of AI
items.takeaway: A longer read on the bigger stakes: what we risk if we let AI flatten how students think, argue, and connect.

items.title: Addressing AI Overwhelm in the Classroom
items.takeaway: A short listen if all of this feels like a lot. You are not the only one.

items.title: Emerging AI Trends in Education for 2026 (w/ Julie Willcott)
items.takeaway: A quick bonus episode on what is coming next year. Good background noise with a payoff.

challenge.title: Find one task you do every week and let AI take the first pass
challenge.body: Not the whole thing. Just the first draft: a parent email, a meeting agenda, a rubric. Spend ten minutes, keep what works, toss what does not, and tell me how it went. The "vibe coding" district up top started exactly here.
